title: Lengths, percentages and viewport units

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Lengths, percentages and viewport units; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Lengths, percentages and viewport units
sources.claim: CSS units express absolute, font-relative, viewport, container, and percentage relationships whose reference depends on the property.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Lengths, percentages and viewport units
sources.claim: CSS units express absolute, font-relative, viewport, container, and percentage relationships whose reference depends on the property.

canonicalExplanation.summary: CSS units express absolute, font-relative, viewport, container, and percentage relationships whose reference depends on the property.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Body text uses rem for user-scalable sizing while a border uses px for a stable one-device-pixel-like design choice. Your goal is to select and justify units for readable text, spacing and responsive dimensions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Rules, declarations and values; Content, padding, border and margin. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: CSS units express absolute, font-relative, viewport, container, and percentage relationships whose reference depends on the property. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Viewport units can interact with browser UI, percentages resolve differently by property, and rem does not follow component-local font size. This draft therefore rejects: rem and em always resolve to the same font-size reference.

includes: Choose relative or absolute units based on typography, container and viewport relationships.
Select and justify units for readable text, spacing and responsive dimensions.
excludes: Viewport units can interact with browser UI, percentages resolve differently by property, and rem does not follow component-local font size.

outcomes: Select and justify units for readable text, spacing and responsive dimensions.

examples.title: Lengths, percentages and viewport units: concrete case
examples.situation: Body text uses rem for user-scalable sizing while a border uses px for a stable one-device-pixel-like design choice.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Body text uses rem for user-scalable sizing while a border uses px for a stable one-device-pixel-like design choice.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: rem references the root font size while em depends on the relevant element font size or property context.
examples.result: The result follows because rem references the root font size while em depends on the relevant element font size or property context.

examples.title: Lengths, percentages and viewport units: boundary case
examples.situation: Viewport units can interact with browser UI, percentages resolve differently by property, and rem does not follow component-local font size.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Viewport units can interact with browser UI, percentages resolve differently by property, and rem does not follow component-local font size.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: rem and em always resolve to the same font-size reference.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: rem references the root font size while em depends on the relevant element font size or property context.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: rem and em always resolve to the same font-size reference.
misconceptions.correction: rem references the root font size while em depends on the relevant element font size or property context.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: rem and em always resolve to the same font-size reference.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Lengths, percentages and viewport units, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Lengths, percentages and viewport units before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to select and justify units for readable text, spacing and responsive dimensions. Keep this boundary in view: Viewport units can interact with browser UI, percentages resolve differently by property, and rem does not follow component-local font size.
